title: Intrinsic Topological Control of the Orbital Hall Effect in Buckled Dirac Materials
authors: Madiha Zia, Muzamil Shah, Kashif Sabeeh, Gao Xianlong, Reza Asgari
abstract: We study the orbital Hall response in buckled two-dimensional Dirac materials using a unified framework that includes an antiferromagnetic exchange field, a perpendicular electric field, and intrinsic spin-orbit coupling. We show that the orbital Hall conductivity is considerably boosted around band-inversion points and shows different signatures across multiple electronic phases using a low-energy massive Dirac model in conjunction with Berry-curvature-based linear response theory. We find a series of quantum spin Hall, valley Hall, and anomalous Hall regimes by methodically adjusting external fields, and demonstrate how the evolution of the orbital response is controlled by the redistribution of Berry curvature between spin and valley sectors. We examine the impacts of finite temperature in more detail and find that although the response s size is suppressed by thermal broadening, the distinctive phase-dependent features remain robust. Our findings demonstrate that orbital Hall conductivity offers a sensitive band topology probe in Dirac systems and emphasize buckled two-dimensional materials as a flexible platform for engineering tunable orbital currents for orbitronic applications.
summary: This paper demonstrates how the Orbital Hall Effect in buckled 2D Dirac materials can be precisely controlled by tuning external electric and magnetic fields. It shows that topological phase transitions, such as the transition between Quantum Spin Hall and Quantum Anomalous Hall phases, lead to significant enhancements in orbital Hall conductivity. These findings suggest that such materials are highly promising platforms for developing tunable orbitronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating the intrinsic topological control of the Orbital Hall Effect (OHE) in buckled 2D Dirac materials using external electric and magnetic fields.
main_result: The orbital Hall conductivity is significantly boosted near band-inversion points and can be tuned across various topological regimes (QSH, QVH, QAH) by adjusting external fields.
method: Berry-curvature-based linear response theory using a low-energy massive Dirac Hamiltonian and Kubo formula calculations.
model_or_system: Buckled two-dimensional Dirac materials (such as the silicene family) modeled by a low-energy massive Dirac Hamiltonian including intrinsic spin-orbit coupling, a perpendicular electric field, and an antiferromagnetic exchange field.
key_observables: Orbital Hall conductivity, Berry curvature, orbital magnetic moment, and orbital angular momentum.
important_parameters: Intrinsic spin-orbit coupling (lambda_SO), perpendicular electric field (Ez), and antiferromagnetic exchange field (lambda_AF).
main_assumptions: The system is in the intrinsic (disorder-free) limit, electrons are treated as independent (neglecting electron-electron interactions), and Rashba spin-orbit coupling is neglected.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of silicene; Figure 2 illustrates band structures for different topological regimes; Figure 3 shows OHC as a function of Fermi energy and temperature; Figure 5 shows OHC vs. tuning parameters; Figure 6 is a phase diagram heatmap; Figure 7 and 8 show the momentum dependence of Berry curvature and orbital magnetic moment.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical system and model, develops the theoretical framework for orbital transport, analyzes the redistribution of Berry curvature across different topological phases, examines the impact of temperature, and concludes with a phase diagram of the orbital response.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. A simple schematic showing a single layer of silicene exposed to an out-of-plane electric field.

FIG. 2. Band structures near the K and K′ valleys for distinct topological regimes. (a) K valley SOC-dominated regime supporting the quantum spin Hall phase. (b) Reduced-gap QSH at Ezℓ= 0.5λSO. (c) Critical point at Ezℓ= λSO where the band gap closes. (d) Quantum spin Hall regime with antiferromagnetic exchange. (e) AF insulator obtained by tuning λAF = λSO. (f) Anomalous Hall phase when both Ezℓand λAF are present. (g) K′ valley SOC-dominated regime supporting the quantum spin Hall phase. (h) Reduced-gap QSH at Ezℓ= 0.5λSO. (i) Critical point at Ezℓ= λSO where the band gap closes. (j) Quantum spin Hall regime with antiferromagnetic exchange. (k) Critical point obtained by tuning λAF = λSO....

FIG. 3. Orbital Hall conductivity σLz yx (in units of e/2π) as a function of EF for distinct phases at different temperatures. Notice that we use T = 0, for the case that T →0 around EF = 0. (a) SOC-dominated regime supporting the quantum spin Hall phase. (b) Reduced-gap QSH at Ezℓ= 0.5λSO. (c) Critical point at Ezℓ= λSO. (d) Quantum spin Hall regime with antiferromagnetic exchange. (e) Critical point at λAF = λSO. (f) Valley Polarized Anomalous Hall phase when both Ezℓ and λAF are present.

FIG. 4. Orbital Hall conductivity σLz yx (in units of e/2π) as a function of EF near the K and K′ valleys for distinct topological regimes. (a) SOC-dominated QSH phase. (b) Reduced-gap QSH at Ezℓ= 0.5λSO. (c) Critical point at Ezℓ= λSO. (d) QSH with antiferromagnetic exchange. (e) AF insulator at λAF = λSO. (f) Valley polarized Anomalous Hall phase.
